title-group.article-title: The application of artificial intelligence in the design of highly compatible knee prostheses: a systematic review

contrib-group.aff: https://ror.org/04wwqze12grid.411642.40000 0004 0605 3760Department of Orthopedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Ministry of Education, Beijing, 100191 China

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: With the rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) technology, it has been widely used in the personalized design of orthopedic implants. Especially in total knee arthroplasty (TKA), AI has shown significant potential in prosthesis size prediction, implant identification and surgical planning. However, the research distribution in this field is scattered, the technical paths are diverse, and there is still a lack of systematic sorting and comprehensive evaluation.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic literature review was conducted to retrieve relevant literature from PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science up to December 2025. Due to substantial heterogeneity across the included studies, a meta-analysis was not performed; instead, a descriptive synthesis approach was adopted. The inclusion criteria covered the application of AI in TKA prosthesis design, size prediction, type identification and surgical planning. Non-knee joint, non-quantitative research and review literature were excluded. A total of 16 studies were included. The descriptive comprehensive method was used to analyze the research characteristics, technical methods, application direction and performance.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 16 studies from 9 countries published between 2019 and 2025 were included. AI has been mainly applied in three directions in TKA prosthesis adaptation: prosthesis size prediction (12 articles), prosthesis type and manufacturer identification (4 articles), surgical planning and personalized design (4 articles). In size prediction, deep learning models, especially CNN, have excellent performance, with accuracy generally between 77 and 91%, and some models can reach more than 99% when allowing ± 1 size error. In the prosthesis recognition task, the model based on EfficientNet, YOLO and other architectures is close to perfect in terms of classification accuracy and AUC. In surgical planning and custom design, AI can automate the whole process from image segmentation to prosthesis generation, and the system processing time can be shortened to less than 15 mins. Despite the positive results, there are common limitations among studies, such as data heterogeneity, sample size differences, and insufficient clinical validation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Preliminary evidence suggests that AI holds promise in improving preoperative planning accuracy and enabling personalized prosthesis design. However, high-level clinical validation is urgently needed to confirm its impact on long-term outcomes such as prosthesis survival and functional recovery.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12891-026-09819-5.

funding-source.institution: Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China
Beijing Municipal Science and Technology Commission, Project for Cultivating Innovative Pharmaceutical Products and Platforms

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Knee osteoarthritis is characterized by progressive articular cartilage wear, osteophyte formation, and synovial inflammation. These pathological changes lead to pain, stiffness, and functional impairment, significantly affecting patients' mobility and quality of life [1]. Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) has emerged as the most effective and well-established surgical treatment for end-stage knee disease. By implanting an artificial prosthesis, TKA aims to alleviate pain, correct deformity, and restore joint stability and mobility [2]. Nevertheless, conventional TKA techniques and prosthesis designs now encounter unprecedented clinical challenges in meeting diverse surgical demands [3].

In current clinical practice, knee prostheses are designed primarily based on the statistical averages of anatomical parameters from specific populations, resulting in a limited series of standardized sizes [4]. However, this “one-size-fits-all” or “limited-selection” approach has inherent shortcomings. Firstly, size mismatch remains a prominent issue. Patients exhibit considerable individual and racial variations in the dimensions, curvature, and torsional angles of the femur, tibia, and patella. Existing prostheses, available only in fixed size intervals, often fail to adequately match the bone morphology of every patient. This may lead to component overhang, incomplete coverage, or insufficient mechanical support—ultimately increasing the risks of postoperative pain, prosthesis loosening, or excessive polyethylene liner wear [5]. Secondly, the reconstruction of the mechanical axis remains imprecise. Restoring the physiological alignment of the lower limb is essential for the success of TKA and the long-term survival of the prosthesis [6]. Conventional surgical techniques, which depend on the surgeon’s experience, two‑dimensional imaging measurements, and mechanical alignment guides, often fail to accurately and individually restore the three‑dimensional lower‑limb mechanical axis, joint line height, and soft‑tissue balance [7]. Even minor alignment deviations can alter intra‑articular stress distribution and accelerate polyethylene wear, contributing to suboptimal postoperative satisfaction and long‑term prosthesis failure. Consequently, achieving a high degree of prosthetic fitting—where the implant conforms closely to the patient’s individual anatomy in shape and reconstructs a personalized, physiological biomechanical environment of the knee—has become a central challenge in improving TKA outcomes [8].

In recent years, artificial intelligence technologies, particularly those based on machine learning and deep learning, have been integrated into healthcare with unprecedented breadth and depth. Within surgery, AI has emerged as a promising enabling tool—from preoperative planning and surgical navigation to robot-assisted procedures—with potential to promote more accurate, minimally invasive, and personalized care [9]. Its core strength lies in the ability to autonomously learn complex patterns and relationships from massive, multimodal medical data, build predictive models, and support precise spatial computation and clinical decision-making. This offers a novel technical paradigm for addressing the challenge of individualized adaptation in knee arthroplasty [10]. By applying deep learning to patient-specific three-dimensional anatomical data, AI can generate a truly “tailor-made” prosthetic design or intelligently identify the optimal implant from a broad range of options. Furthermore, through AI-driven surgical planning and robotic execution, virtual personalized plans can be seamlessly translated into reality [11].

While previous reviews have broadly summarized AI applications in orthopedics, the present review offers a distinct contribution by focusing specifically on prosthesis design and fit—a critical determinant of TKA outcomes. We systematically categorize the emerging literature into three application paradigms: size prediction, implant identification, and personalized design. Importantly, our analysis extends beyond reporting technical performance metrics to critically evaluate underexplored dimensions that are essential for clinical translation: model interpretability, robustness testing, data heterogeneity, clinical validation levels, workflow integration, and regulatory considerations. By synthesizing both technical achievements and implementation barriers, this review provides a comprehensive roadmap for future research and clinical adoption.

sec.title: Literature search strategy
sec.p: This systematic review was reported following the guidelines of Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews. The literature search was conducted through December 2025 and covered the following databases: PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science. In PubMed, the search was performed using a combination of MeSH terms and free-text words, with field restrictions applied to title and abstract where appropriate: ("arthroplasty, replacement, knee"[Mesh] OR "knee prosthesis"[Mesh] OR "total knee arthroplasty"[Title/Abstract] OR "knee prosthesis"[Title/Abstract] OR "TKA implant"[Title/Abstract]) AND ("artificial intelligence"[Mesh] OR "artificial intelligence"[Title/Abstract] OR "deep learning"[Title/Abstract] OR "machine learning"[Title/Abstract] OR "convolutional neural network"[Title/Abstract]). In Scopus, the search was limited to titles, abstracts, and keywords using the following syntax: (TITLE-ABS-KEY ("total knee arthroplasty") OR TITLE-ABS-KEY ("knee prosthesis") OR TITLE-ABS-KEY ("TKA implant")) AND (TITLE-ABS-KEY ("artificial intelligence") OR TITLE-ABS-KEY ("deep learning") OR TITLE-ABS-KEY ("machine learning") OR TITLE-ABS-KEY ("convolutional neural network")). In Web of Science, the search was conducted using the topic field (title, abstract, and keywords) with the following syntax: TS = ("total knee arthroplasty" OR "knee prosthesis" OR "TKA implant") AND TS = ("artificial intelligence" OR "deep learning" OR "machine learning" OR "convolutional neural network"). The literature search was conducted on January 15, 2026, and search time frame was up to December 2025 to cover the main progress since the rise of artificial intelligence in the field of knee prosthesis design. To minimize publication bias, we also searched grey literature sources including Google Scholar and preprint servers (medRxiv, arXiv); however, no studies meeting the inclusion criteria were identified from these sources.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
Literature screening and data extraction
sec.p: Literature screening is divided into three stages: Firstly, duplicate literature is removed using EndNote software; After duplicate removal, two reviewers (K.L. and T.Z.) independently screened the titles, abstracts, and full texts against the inclusion and exclusion criteria. The reviewers were not blinded to the authors’ names, institutions, or journal sources. Disagreements were resolved by discussion or by consultation with a third reviewer (X.W.). Data extraction uses a standardized table, which includes: authors, publication year, country, sample size, research purpose, AI architecture, input data type, outcome indicators, main results and conclusions, etc.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: Two reviewers (K.L. and T.Z.) independently assessed the risk of bias for each included study. Given the heterogeneity of study designs—including retrospective cohort studies, prospective controlled trials, and technical validation studies—we employed two complementary assessment tools. For non-randomized studies of interventions (e.g., retrospective and prospective comparative studies), we used the ROBINS-I (Risk Of Bias In Non-randomized Studies of Interventions) tool, which evaluates bias across seven domains: (1) confounding, (2) selection of participants, (3) classification of interventions, (4) deviations from intended interventions, (5) missing data, (6) measurement of outcomes, and (7) selection of the reported result. Each domain was rated as low risk of bias (+), moderate risk of bias (?), serious risk of bias (×), or no information (NI). Disagreements between reviewers were resolved through discussion or by consultation with a third reviewer (X.W.).

sec.title: Data integration and analysis
sec.p: Through comparison and induction, the technical development trends, common challenges, and research gaps in this field were summarized, providing a basis for subsequent discussions and outlooks. In this review, “prosthesis compatibility” is operationalized based on the reported outcomes in primary studies: primarily anatomical fit (e.g., size prediction accuracy, bone coverage, overhang, alignment errors), and secondarily functional compatibility (e.g., correlation with clinical outcome scores such as WOMAC and KSS) where available.

sec.title: Literature selection
sec.p:
Figure 1 Outlines the article selection process for this scoping review. Initially, the electronic literature search revealed 4369 records, and after eliminating duplicate articles, 2134 unique records were obtained in the initial database search for screening titles and abstracts, of which 2098 were excluded. 36 studies were reviewed in more detail, of which 16 were excluded because they did not report outcome measures and 4 were excluded because they focused on non-knee joints (Fig. 1). Finally, 16 eligible papers were included in the qualitative review (Table 1). The group unanimously agreed on literature selection and publication classification. The risk-of-bias assessment is summarized in Table 2. Overall, most studies demonstrated a low to moderate risk of bias, primarily due to retrospective data collection and lack of external validation. No studies were excluded based solely on bias assessment, but the findings are interpreted with consideration of these limitationsFig. 1
Flowchart of literature screening process. Records were identified from PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science. After duplicate removal, titles and abstracts were screened, yielding the records for full-text review. Full-text articles were excluded for the following reasons: not reporting outcome measure, non-knee joint focus, and other reasons. The final analysis included 16 studiesTable 1
The basic information of the included literature
AuthorYearCountrySample SizeProposeMain Results/Conclusion
Burge2022UK78Using X-rays to predict the most suitable size of total knee replacement implantsThe average accuracy rate for predicting the size of the femoral component was 77.95%, while that for the tibial side was 80.51%
Yue2022China308Predicting the size of knee joint prostheses under unmarked X-ray imagingThe prediction accuracy rates for the femoral components and tibial components reached 88.23% and 86.27% respectively
Kunze2021USA17,283Using demographic variables to achieve accurate prediction of TKA component sizesThe accuracy rate of the model in predicting the sizes of the femur and tibia was 83.6% and 83.0%
Guezou2025France75Design a fully automated workflow for orthopedic implants specifically for patientsThe workflow can quickly and reliably customize personalized knee joint implants
Mulford2025USA9651Develop a deep learning algorithm to automatically identify the most common primary TKA implant modelsThe average model accuracy rate was 99.7%
Park2024Korea234Utilizing deep learning to assist in determining the sizes of femoral and tibial implantsThe accuracy rate of the model in predicting the sizes of femoral and tibial implants was 89.32% and 90.60%
Klemt2022USA8763Identification of the design of total hip and knee joint prosthesis implantsThe deep learning model has extremely high accuracy in identifying 24 sets of THA and 14 sets of TKA designs
Vogl2022Switzerland106,000Perform personalized pose estimation from single-planar moving perspective images using deep convolutional neural networksThe trained deep learning model achieved full automation of pose estimation
Kunze2022USA11,777Predicting the size of the TKA implantThe machine learning algorithm achieved an accuracy ranging from good to extremely high in predicting the tibia and femur for TKA
Farooq2021USA1091Identifying the optimal position of the sagittal plane components during total knee arthroplastyThe robust, accurate and automated full knee joint replacement customization process based on CT has been proven feasible
Burge2023UK98Apply machine learning methods to automatically customize knee replacement implants from CT dataThe robust, accurate and automated custom process for total knee replacement based on CT has been proven feasible
Jang2023USA235Evaluate implant fixation in a standardized mannerThe deep learning algorithm can objectively and standardly automatically delineate the correction areas and cone positions of postoperative rTKA radiographs
Lan2024China60Predicting the size and axial arrangement of the implantsThe preoperative three-dimensional planning technology based on artificial intelligence in TKA has a more reliable planning effect on the size and axial arrangement of the prosthesis
Twinprai2025Thailand3228Develop an artificial intelligence system that can classify the types of knee joint prostheses using ordinary X-ray imagesThe AI model shows excellent performance indicators, with accuracy, recall rate and precision all being 100%
Karnuta2021USA424Accurately identifying the manufacturer and model of the implants for patients undergoing reoperationThe deep learning algorithm perfectly distinguishes 9 different knee joint implantations from four manufacturers
Yu2024Korea714Predicting the size of implants for total knee replacement surgery using X-ray imagesThe femoral micro F1 score is 0.91, and the tibial score is 0.87Table 2
Risk of bias assessment

D1 Bias due to confounding
D2 Bias in selection of participants
D3 Bias in classification of interventions
D4 Bias due to deviations from intended interventions
D5 Bias due to missing data
D6 Bias in measurement of outcomes
D7 Bias in selection of the reported result
+ low risk of bias;? moderate risk of bias; × serious risk of bias; NI no information

sec.title: Study of basic characteristics
sec.p: This study included a total of 16 studies, covering relevant literature published between 2019 and 2025. These studies mainly originated from the United States (6), China (3), the United Kingdom (2), South Korea (2), Switzerland, France, and Thailand, each with one study. The sample sizes varied significantly, ranging from the minimum of 60 cases to the maximum of 106,000 cases. Most of the studies focused on samples of several hundred to several thousand cases, and most of them used clinical or imaging data for modeling and validation. The included studies exhibited substantial heterogeneity. Sample sizes ranged from 60 to 106,000 cases. Imaging modalities varied (X-ray: 12 studies; CT: 3 studies; fluoroscopy: 1 study), and evaluation metrics were inconsistent across studies—size prediction studies primarily reported accuracy (77%–91%) or MAE/RMSE, while implant identification studies uniformly reported accuracy, AUC, and F1 scores.

sec.title: Characteristics of the AI architecture system
sec.p:
The AI architectures employed in the included literature exhibit diverse characteristics (Table 3). From the perspective of technological evolution, deep learning architectures have become the mainstream, especially convolutional neural networks (CNNs) which dominate in image recognition tasks (9 studies adopted this approach). It is notable that the network structure shows a trend towards lightweighting and specialization: EfficientNet is favored for its balance between parameter efficiency and accuracy [14]; U-Net performs well in medical image segmentation task [21]; ResNet-101 demonstrates advantages in complex feature extraction [24]. Meanwhile, traditional machine learning models remain competitive in multivariate prediction tasks, especially when dealing with structured clinical data. Ensemble learning methods such as random forest (RF) and eXtreme gradient boosting (XGB) models are listed as benchmark models or main methods in multiple studies [13, 18]. We systematically categorize the included studies into three distinct application domains: size prediction, implant identification, and personalized design. This classification framework allows for a structured analysis of technical approaches, performance, and clinical relevance.Table 3
The artificial intelligence information of the included literature
Author/YearAI architectureModel InputEvaluation IndicatorsAI Application
Burge
2022 [31]CNNX-rayCompared with the truly optimal plan determined based on the MRI data of the subjectsPrediction of implant size for TKA
Yue

Kunze

Guezou

Mulford

Park
2024 [15]CNN and deep learningX-ray, Surgical RecordThe accuracy between the predicted size and the actual sizePrediction of implant dimensions for total knee replacement surgery
Klemt
2022 [16]CNNX-rayThe area under the curve (AUC), sensitivity and specificityAutomatically identify the most common primary TKA implant models
Vogl
2022 [17]Deep Auto-Match NetworkX-rayin-plane translation, out-of-plane translation, all Euler-angle rotationsPersonalized posture estimation of the knee joint
Kunze
2022 [18]machine-learningAge, gender, height, weight and body mass indexAccuracy, MAE and RMSEPrediction within the size range of the implants for TKA
Farooq
2021 [19]machine-learningX-raysatisfaction scores、Knee Society ScorePredicting the optimal position of the sagittal plane components
Burge
2023 [20]machine-learningCTvirtually fitting outputted implant designs with 'ground truth' 3D models for each test subject's bonesAutomatic Design of Knee Joint Implants
Jang
2023 [21]U-netX-raydice segmentation coefficient, accuracy metricsEvaluation of implant fixation
Lan
2024 [26]G-NETCT、X-rayWOMAC and AKS scores, prosthesis size, varus correction angle and hip-knee-ankle anglePrediction of implant size and axial arrangement
Twinprai
2025 [22]YOLOv9 model MX-rayPrecision, recall (sensitivity), accuracy, F1 score and receiver operating characteristic curveAutomatically identify the most common primary TKA implant models
Karnuta
2021 [23]CNNX-rayAUC, sensitivity, specificity and accuracy of the subjectsAutomatically identify the most common primary TKA implant models
Yu
2024 [24]ResNet-101X-rayThe training accuracy and F1 score of the modelAutomatically predict the size of the TKA prosthesis

sec.title: Input data dimensions and types
sec.p:
The input data used in the literature exhibit a multi-modal fusion characteristic:
Imaging data: 12 studies used X-ray images as the core input, and 3 studies employed CT images as the input. Particularly in tasks of 3D modeling and precise measuremen [25, 26]. Vogl’s study used single-plane moving perspective images with manually annotated postures, demonstrating the need for dynamic information [17].
Clinical variables: 2 studies integrated demographic and clinical measurement data, mainly including age, gender, height, weight, and BMI indicators. Such data are usually input in parallel with imaging data or as auxiliary features [13, 18].
Surgery-related data: Park (2024) and Farooq (2021)’s studies included surgical records and patient-reported outcomes as input features, reflecting the evolution from pure imaging analysis to a clinical decision support system [15, 19].

sec.title: Model validation and evaluation system
sec.p:
The evaluation system of the research presents multi-dimensional and multi-level characteristics:
Classification task evaluation: The research on prosthetic type identification mainly adopts accuracy rate, precision rate, recall rate, F1 score, and AUC-ROC curve. Twinprai (2025) and Karnuta (2021)also provided specificity and sensitivity analysis [22, 23].
Regression task evaluation: For size prediction research, in addition to accuracy rate, mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) are widely used as main indicators (Kunze, 2021,2022) [13, 18]. Lan (2024)'s research further included angle measurement errors (such as VCA, HKA) [26].

Segmentation task evaluation: Research based on medical image segmentation (Jang, 2023) uses volume similarity indicators such as Dice segmentation coefficient and Jaccard index [21].
Clinical relevance evaluation: Some prospective studies (Farooq, 2021; Lan, 2024) correlated the AI prediction results with clinical outcome indicators (such as Knee Society Score, WOMAC score), establishing a bridge between technical performance and clinical effectiveness [19, 26].

sec.title: Training strategies and technical details
sec.p: Training-validation split: Most studies adopt a training-validation-test set division ratio of 7:2:1 or 8:1:1. Loss function selection: For classification tasks, cross-entropy loss is commonly used; for regression tasks, mean squared error (MSE) or mean absolute error (MAE) are often employed; for segmentation tasks, Dice loss or its variants are used. Optimizer configuration: Adam and its variants are the commonly used optimization algorithms, and the learning rate is mostly set with a stepwise decay strategy. It is worth noting that some studies have begun to explore end-to-end full-process systems. The research by Burge (2023) and Guezou-Philippe (2025)established a complete AI workflow from image acquisition, bone segmentation, morphological analysis to prosthesis design, achieving “image input—prosthesis output” automated processing [20, 25].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: With the deep penetration of artificial intelligence technology in the field of orthopedics, its application in TKA prosthesis adaptation has gradually moved from early exploration to systematization and practicality. This study systematically reviewed 16 related studies in recent years, covering three major directions: prosthesis size prediction, prosthesis type identification, and surgical planning and design. The following discussion combines the main results with insights on technical challenges and future perspectives.

In general, artificial intelligence has shown high accuracy, repeatability and clinical translation potential in knee prosthesis adaptation. In terms of prosthesis size prediction, the accuracy of most studies exceeded 80%, and some models could reach more than 99% when allowing ± 1 size error, which was significantly higher than the traditional manual template measurement (such as 61.54%−68.38% reported by Park et al.). [27] In the task of prosthesis type and manufacturer identification, the deep learning model showed nearly perfect classification ability (AUC 0.97–1.00), especially with good generalization performance under the support of large samples of multicenter data. In the aspect of surgical planning and implant design, AI has been able to realize automatic processes from image segmentation, bone reconstruction to personalized prosthesis generation. Some systems can complete tasks that traditionally take hours in 15 min, showing good workflow integration ability.

sec.title: Prosthesis size prediction: towards precision and automation
sec.p: This direction aims to predict the required size of femoral and tibial prosthesis by AI models before operation, which is the most concentrated research field. High prediction accuracy (more than 80%) and clinical practicability (accuracy within ± 1 scale is often used as the key indicator) are generally pursued in this direction. The evolution of technology shows a clear trajectory from relying on traditional statistical features to based on depth analysis of medical images. Early studies mainly use structured clinical data such as age, gender, height, weight, BMI, and other machine learning algorithms such as random forest, support vector machine, gradient boosting [13, 18]. The advantages of such methods are easy access to data and some interpretability of models (e.g., Kunze's study showed that sex and height are the most important predictors), but their prediction ceiling is limited by the representability of features. In recent years, image-driven methods with CNN as the core have become the absolute mainstream [12, 20, 24, 27]. CNN can automatically extract rich morphological and anatomical features directly from X-ray or CT images, which is more suitable for the actual needs of surgical planning.

Burge (2022)’s research constructed a complete 2D-3D pipeline, which was unique in that it not only predicted dimensions but also performed virtual fitting for different types of prostheses. It recommended the best solution by calculating biomechanical indicators such as bone coverage and overhang, directly linking AI predictions with the final implant outcome [31].

Yue (2022) addressed the classic problem of small sample size and class imbalance in medical imaging, innovatively introducing the Error-Correcting Output Coding (ECOC) framework to optimize the model. It achieved excellent performance on a medium sample size (n= 308) and provided a technical solution for addressing data bottlenecks [12].

Kunze (2021, 2022)'s two studies, leveraging ultra-large-scale, multi-center clinical datasets (n > 10,000), rigorously verified the efficacy of traditional machine learning models in this task and developed a web application prototype for real-time clinical queries, highlighting efforts to transform into clinical tools [13, 18].

Park (2024)'s research design has direct clinical significance. It compared the prediction results of the DL model with the manual template measurement results of experienced surgeons head-to-head, demonstrating the value of AI-assisted decision-making with a significantly higher accuracy rate (~ 90% vs. ~ 65%) by providing strong evidence [27].

Yu (2024) emphasized the purity of its model, using only preoperative anteroposterior X-ray images and not relying on any demographic data. It simplified the data input requirements and demonstrated the powerful ability of deep networks to learn size information from images. Its Micro-F1 score (femur 0.91, tibia 0.87) reflects its robustness in multi-classification tasks [24].

sec.title: Implant type and manufacturer identification: empowering revision surgery planning
sec.p: This direction focuses on automatically identifying the brand and model of the implanted prosthesis from X-ray images, which is crucial for the preoperative planning of revision surgeries [28]. This task is essentially a high-precision image classification problem, so the research almost exclusively uses advanced deep learning classification models such as CNN, EfficientNet, ResNet, and the object detection framework YOLO. The evaluation metrics are highly consistent, all aiming for an accuracy, recall rate, and AUC value close to 100%. The research is generally based on large-scale postoperative X-ray databases for model training and validation.

Klemt (2022) and Karnuta (2021)conducted earlier systematic studies to verify the extremely high feasibility of the CNN model in this task, which can distinguish up to dozens of different hip and knee prosthesis designs, with an AUC value of 0.97–0.99, laying the confidence for the field [16, 23].

Mulford (2025) further advanced in engineering and safety. It not only processed over a million images using the efficient EfficientNet architecture, but more importantly, introduced conformal prediction to provide uncertainty estimation and designed an outlier detection system that can automatically alert images beyond the model's cognitive range, which is a crucial safety mechanism in actual clinical deployment [14].

Twinprai (2025) demonstrated the outstanding potential of lightweight models (YOLOv9) in this task, achieving perfect performance of all indicators at 100% in the classification of seven prostheses, and suggesting the possibility of this technology being deployed on mobile devices or edge computing [22].

sec.title: Surgical planning and personalized implant design: From automation to true personalization
sec.p: This is the most forward-looking direction, aiming to utilize AI to go beyond “selecting existing models” and directly participate in or complete “designing customized prostheses” and “planning surgical procedures”. This research direction typically involves complex multi-step processes and integrates various AI technologies. The core steps include: 1) using segmentation networks such as U-Net to perform high-precision bone segmentation on CT/X-ray images; 2) reconstructing the three-dimensional bone model from the segmentation results through statistical shape models or neural networks; 3) conducting morphometric analysis to extract design parameters; 4) finally, outputting prosthetic design schemes or surgical planning recommendations through rule engines or generative design. The evaluation indicators are diverse, including segmentation accuracy (Dice coefficient), anatomical landmark error, design fit, and long-term clinical functional scores.

Guezou-Philippe (2025) and Burge (2023)’s research represent cutting-edge exploration of fully automated customized design. The former proposed a complete, un-intervened 15-min workflow from CT segmentation to implant design; the latter developed an automated pipeline based on CT, integrating multiple ML models for classification, detection, and segmentation. Both have demonstrated the technical feasibility and significant potential in terms of time and cost of AI-driven personalized prosthetic customization [20, 25].

Jang (2023)’s research addresses the clinical challenges in evaluating TKA revision surgery, developing an algorithm using U-Net that can automatically delineate fixed areas on X-ray films and identify reinforcing cones. Its contribution lies in applying AI to the standardized and objective assessment of postoperative efficacy, providing new tools for long-term follow-up and research [21].

Lan (2024)’s research conducted a rigorous prospective clinical controlled trial, comparing the AI-based three-dimensional planning technique with traditional two-dimensional template measurements. The results not only showed that the AI group was more accurate in predicting prosthetic size and alignment, but more importantly, achieved significantly better WOMAC and AKS functional scores at 3, 6, and 12 months post-surgery, providing early empirical evidence that AI planning technology can bring better clinical outcomes [26].

Farooq (2021)’s perspective is unique. It uses machine learning to reverse-analyze clinical outcome data, seeking the “sweet spot” of sagittal plane prosthetic position related to patient satisfaction. This data-driven approach provides new evidence for defining surgical goals and elevates AI from an execution tool to a decision discovery tool [19].

Although direct quantitative comparisons across studies are precluded by heterogeneous datasets and tasks, a qualitative synthesis reveals emerging trends. Lightweight architectures achieved near-perfect classification performance in implant identification tasks while maintaining computational efficiency, suggesting their suitability for deployment on edge devices or within PACS systems. In contrast, deeper architectures such as ResNet-101 and U-Net were predominantly used for tasks requiring complex feature extraction, such as bone segmentation and 3D reconstruction. End-to-end systems demonstrated the feasibility of fully automated workflows, reducing processing time to under 15 min. However, quantitative assessments of deployment costs, hardware requirements, and computational efficiency remain largely absent, limiting practical guidance for clinical implementation. Furthermore, many included studies lacked comprehensive reporting of training details, and none provided shared code or datasets. This limits reproducibility and highlights the need for adherence to standardized reporting guidelines.

Successful clinical translation of AI-driven prosthesis design requires a structured pathway encompassing multiple stages [29]. First, validation must progress from internal validation to external validation across multiple institutions, and ultimately to prospective clinical trials that demonstrate improvements in clinically meaningful outcomes. Second, workflow integration demands interoperability with existing hospital infrastructure, as well as consideration of surgeon learning curves and hardware costs—factors currently unexplored in the literature. Third, regulatory approval requires adherence to frameworks such as FDA’s Software as a Medical Device pathway, with clear documentation of safety, efficacy, and risk management. Fourth, post-deployment monitoring should incorporate uncertainty estimation and human-in-the-loop mechanisms to ensure safe use. Addressing these translational steps is essential to bridge the gap between technical innovation and clinical practice [30].

Despite the remarkable achievements, the current research still faces several common problems: 1) Insufficient data quality and standardization: The imaging acquisition devices, protocols, and annotation standards used in various studies are inconsistent, and most data sets have limited sample sizes and insufficient population representativeness, which affects the generalization ability of the models. 2) Delay in clinical validation and translation: Most studies are still in the retrospective validation stage and lack prospective, multi-center clinical trials to prove their effectiveness and safety in actual surgeries. 3) Lack of model interpretability: Deep learning models mostly operate as black boxes, making it difficult for doctors to understand the basis for their decisions, which may affect clinical adoption in high-risk medical scenarios. 4) System integration and workflow obstacles: How AI tools can be seamlessly integrated into existing hospital information systems, imaging archiving and surgical planning platforms still faces technical and administrative challenges. 5) Although we searched grey literature sources (Google Scholar, medRxiv, arXiv), no eligible studies were identified. The exclusion of non-English publications and unpublished studies may have introduced publication bias, potentially overestimating the reported performance of AI models. 6) Current studies predominantly report conventional performance metrics but lack critical analyses of model interpretability and robustness. None of the included studies employed explainability techniques to elucidate which anatomical features drove model predictions, leaving the decision-making process opaque. Furthermore, robustness testing—such as evaluating model performance under image noise, artifacts, or anatomical variations—was absent across all studies. This “black box” behavior and lack of stress testing undermine clinical confidence and represent a significant barrier to real-world deployment.

Beyond technical and clinical validation, the deployment of AI models in total knee arthroplasty requires robust regulatory and ethical frameworks. None of the included studies discussed AI medical device approval pathways (e.g., FDA Software as a Medical Device), data privacy protections, or liability allocation for surgical errors potentially attributable to AI mispredictions.

Additionally, our literature search was limited to three English-language databases (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science) and did not include Chinese databases such as CNKI or Wanfang. We also did not systematically search conference proceedings or dissertations. These limitations may have resulted in the omission of relevant studies, particularly from regions with substantial contributions to this field, and may have introduced language and publication bias. Furthermore, few included studies provided biomechanical testing or long-term clinical follow-up to validate the functional performance of AI-driven designs.

Looking to the future, the following steps should be taken: 1) Promote cross-institutional data sharing and standardization: Establish an open and diverse database for knee joint images and prostheses, and formulate unified standards for image acquisition, annotation, and evaluation. 2) Integrate multimodal and dynamic information: Combine dynamic data such as gait analysis and joint dynamics to build a “structure–function” joint prediction model, enabling a transition from static anatomical fit to dynamic functional optimization. 3) Explainable AI and human–machine collaboration: Develop a visual decision support interface, highlighting the key anatomical regions inferred by AI, and enhancing doctors' trust and collaboration with the system. 4) Prospective clinical validation and real-world research: Promote the evaluation of AI tools in multi-center, randomized controlled trials, and gradually conduct long-term follow-ups to clarify their impact on postoperative function, prosthesis survival rate, and patient-reported outcomes. 5) Construction of regulatory and ethical frameworks: As the approval process for AI medical devices improves, it is necessary to clarify responsibility attribution, data privacy protection, and algorithm transparency requirements, promoting its compliant implementation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Artificial intelligence demonstrates considerable potential to reshape the prosthetic fitting paradigm for TKA, with preliminary evidence supporting its accuracy, efficiency, and clinical feasibility. However, further breakthroughs in data standardization, clinical validation, and system integration are needed before widespread clinical adoption.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Kangpeng Li, Ti Zhang and Xinguang Wang contributed equally to this work.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors thank Peking University Health Science Center for providing the literature search service.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Kangpeng Li, Ti Zhang, Xinguang Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Funding, Writing –original draft. Feng Li, Hua Tian: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Funding, Writing – review & editing.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This study was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2024YFB3814700); National Natural Science Foundation of China (82372450); Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality (L234012, L254010); Beijing Municipal Science and Technology Commission, Project for Cultivating Innovative Pharmaceutical Products and Platforms (2241100009024023).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No new data were generated or analysed during this study. All data supporting the findings of this study are available from the published articles cited in the reference list.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
